questions.explanation: Correct answer: B. Vitamin A excess is associated with intracranial hypertension (pseudotumor cerebri), hepatotoxicity, and teratogenicity. A is opposite. C points to vitamin K or E interactions. D indicates B12 deficiency. Clinical relevance: strict caution with retinoids in women of childbearing age.
questions.source: NEET PG Advanced Vitamins and Minerals Integration

questions.questionText: A supplement user presents with nausea, polyuria, constipation, and confusion; labs show hypercalcemia with suppressed PTH. Most likely vitamin toxicity is:
questions.optionA: Vitamin D excess causing increased intestinal calcium absorption
questions.optionB: Vitamin C excess causing oxalate nephropathy only
questions.optionD: Biotin excess causing pseudohypocalcemia
questions.explanation: Correct answer: A. Hypervitaminosis D causes hypercalcemia and hypercalciuria with low PTH. B may occur but does not explain this hormonal profile. C and D are incorrect for this presentation. Clinical relevance: key toxicity differential in patients overusing supplements.
questions.source: NEET PG Advanced Vitamins and Minerals Integration
questions.conceptTag: Vitamin D toxicity hypercalcemia suppressed PTH

questions.questionText: Which adverse profile is most characteristic of prolonged high-dose pyridoxine therapy?
questions.optionA: Hemolysis with Heinz bodies
questions.optionB: Irreversible optic neuritis with central scotoma
questions.optionC: Painful symmetric sensory neuropathy from dorsal root ganglion toxicity
questions.optionD: Nephrogenic diabetes insipidus
questions.explanation: Correct answer: B. Chronic excess vitamin B6 can cause sensory neuropathy, unlike deficiency which causes neuropathy and seizures via PLP pathways. A, C, and D are not classic pyridoxine toxicity patterns. Clinical relevance: both deficiency and toxicity of B6 are testable and clinically important.
questions.source: NEET PG Advanced Vitamins and Minerals Integration

questions.questionText: A dyslipidemic patient on niacin develops flushing and worsening glycemic control. Which mediator-target pair best explains the flushing component?
questions.optionA: Histamine release blocked by H1 antagonists
questions.optionB: Bradykinin release blocked by ACE inhibitors
questions.optionD: Prostaglandin-mediated vasodilation via GPR109A pathway, partially attenuated by aspirin
questions.explanation: Correct answer: C. Niacin flushing is largely prostaglandin-mediated and can be reduced with aspirin pretreatment. A, B, and D do not represent the principal mechanism. Clinical relevance: explains counseling around niacin adherence and adverse effects including hyperglycemia and hyperuricemia.
questions.source: NEET PG Advanced Vitamins and Minerals Integration

questions.questionText: A newborn with no prophylaxis develops day-3 mucosal and umbilical stump bleeding; PT is prolonged with normal platelet count. Most likely pathogenesis is:
questions.optionA: Failure of vitamin K-dependent gamma-carboxylation due to low neonatal stores and sterile gut
questions.optionB: Congenital factor VIII deficiency due to maternal folate deficiency
questions.optionD: Vitamin C deficiency causing collagen capillary fragility
questions.explanation: Correct answer: C. Vitamin K deficiency bleeding of the newborn occurs because neonatal stores are low, gut flora are limited, and breast milk vitamin K is low; vitamin K-dependent clotting factors are undercarboxylated. A and B do not match this PT-dominant pattern. D is unrelated. Clinical relevance: universal prophylactic vitamin K at birth prevents VKDB.
questions.source: NEET PG Advanced Vitamins and Minerals Integration
questions.conceptTag: VKDB newborn vitamin K gamma carboxylation

questions.questionText: All are correctly matched regarding vitamin-derived coenzymes EXCEPT:
questions.optionA: Pantothenate (B5) - coenzyme A for acyl-group transfer
questions.optionB: Riboflavin (B2) - FAD or FMN in oxidoreductase reactions
questions.optionD: Niacin (B3) - NAD or NADP for redox transfer
questions.explanation: Correct answer (EXCEPT): one-carbon transfer as THF is a folate (B9) function, not B5. A, B, and C are correct mappings. Clinical relevance: vitamin-to-coenzyme mismatches are common exam traps in metabolism integration.
questions.source: NEET PG Final Vitamins and Minerals Integration
questions.conceptTag: B5 coenzyme A mapping versus folate one carbon trap

questions.questionText: A mutation in a steroid hormone receptor causes loss of DNA binding despite preserved ligand binding. Which micronutrient-linked motif is most likely affected?
questions.optionA: Copper-containing heme domain
questions.optionB: Zinc-finger DNA-binding domain stabilized by cysteine residues
questions.optionD: Iron-sulfur cluster of mitochondrial complex I
questions.explanation: Correct answer: B. Many nuclear receptors and transcription factors use zinc-finger motifs for sequence-specific DNA binding. A, C, and D do not represent the canonical receptor DNA-binding motif. Clinical relevance: links zinc biology to gene expression and endocrine signaling.
questions.source: NEET PG Final Vitamins and Minerals Integration
questions.conceptTag: Zinc finger transcription factor DNA binding

questions.questionText: Which transport pairing is most accurate in nutrient biochemistry?
questions.optionA: 25-hydroxyvitamin D mainly binds vitamin D-binding protein; B12 circulates on transcobalamin II; iron on transferrin
questions.optionB: Vitamin D in plasma mainly bound to albumin only
questions.optionC: B12 after ileal uptake circulates chiefly on transferrin
questions.optionD: Ceruloplasmin is the principal plasma iron transporter
questions.explanation: Correct answer: D. 25-OH vitamin D binds DBP, B12 circulates on transcobalamins (biologically active fraction on transcobalamin II), and iron is transported by transferrin. A, B, and C are incorrect pairings. Clinical relevance: high-yield transport-protein comparison trap.
questions.source: NEET PG Final Vitamins and Minerals Integration
questions.conceptTag: Carrier proteins DBP transcobalamin transferrin comparison

questions.questionText: A child has bowed legs, widened wrists, and delayed dentition; another adult has diffuse bone pain with Looser zones; a third has gum bleeding and perifollicular hemorrhage. Which pairing is correct?
questions.optionA: Rickets - vitamin C deficiency; osteomalacia - vitamin K deficiency; scurvy - vitamin D deficiency
questions.optionB: Rickets and osteomalacia are both due to copper deficiency, while scurvy is folate deficiency
questions.optionC: All three are primarily due to B12 deficiency with impaired DNA synthesis
questions.optionD: Rickets and osteomalacia reflect defective mineralization from vitamin D axis failure, while scurvy reflects defective collagen hydroxylation
questions.explanation: Correct answer: C. Rickets (children) and osteomalacia (adults) are mineralization disorders classically linked to vitamin D-calcium-phosphate axis disturbance; scurvy is defective collagen maturation from vitamin C deficiency. A, B, and D are incorrect etiologies. Clinical relevance: frequent clinicopathologic differentiation stem.
questions.source: NEET PG Final Vitamins and Minerals Integration

questions.questionText: In calcium homeostasis, calcitonin is best described as:
questions.optionA: A hormone from thyroid C cells that can reduce osteoclast-mediated resorption, but PTH and vitamin D are the major chronic regulators
questions.optionB: A dominant day-to-day regulator stronger than PTH and calcitriol in adults
questions.optionD: A parathyroid-derived hormone that increases phosphate reabsorption
questions.explanation: Correct answer: B. Calcitonin is produced by thyroid parafollicular C cells and opposes osteoclastic bone resorption; however, chronic calcium-phosphate regulation is primarily by PTH and calcitriol. A overstates calcitonin importance in adults. C and D misidentify source and actions. Clinical relevance: endocrine integration trap in bone-mineral questions.
questions.source: NEET PG Final Vitamins and Minerals Integration
questions.conceptTag: Calcitonin versus PTH vitamin D homeostasis hierarchy

questions.questionText: Broad-spectrum antibiotic therapy in a malnourished inpatient is followed by rising PT with normal platelet count. Most likely mechanism is:
questions.optionA: Antibiotics directly inhibit factor VIII synthesis
questions.optionB: Antibiotics increase VKORC1 activity causing resistance to vitamin K
questions.optionC: Antibiotics acutely deplete intrinsic factor causing B12-dependent coagulopathy
questions.optionD: Suppression of intestinal flora reduces menaquinone contribution, worsening vitamin K-dependent factor undercarboxylation
questions.explanation: Correct answer: A. Gut microbiota contribute vitamin K (especially K2), and antibiotics can precipitate deficiency in susceptible patients, prolonging PT via reduced gamma-carboxylation of factors II, VII, IX, X. B, C, and D are incorrect. Clinical relevance: classic ward-based drug-vitamin interaction question.
questions.source: NEET PG Final Vitamins and Minerals Integration
questions.conceptTag: Antibiotics vitamin K deficiency PT prolongation mechanism

questions.questionText: Long-term oral contraceptive use is associated with irritability and neuropathic symptoms partly because of:
questions.optionA: Direct depletion of vitamin A retinal stores only
questions.optionB: Functional reduction in pyridoxine-dependent pathways affecting neurotransmitter synthesis
questions.optionC: Inhibition of hepcidin causing severe iron overload
questions.optionD: Selective inactivation of biotinidase
questions.explanation: Correct answer: A. Oral contraceptives have been associated with altered B6 status in some settings, potentially affecting PLP-dependent neurotransmitter metabolism. B, C, and D are not the expected mechanism. Clinical relevance: integrates pharmacology with micronutrient neurochemistry.
questions.source: NEET PG Final Vitamins and Minerals Integration

questions.questionText: In iron absorption and export, which sequence is correct?
questions.optionA: Fe3+ enters enterocyte through DMT1, then ferritin exports iron through transferrin
questions.optionB: Heme iron is absorbed unchanged into plasma and does not require ferroportin
questions.optionC: Dietary non-heme Fe3+ is reduced to Fe2+, imported by DMT1, exported by ferroportin, then oxidized for transferrin loading
questions.optionD: Hepcidin activates ferroportin to increase plasma iron during inflammation
questions.explanation: Correct answer: C. Non-heme iron is reduced to Fe2+ at the brush border, enters via DMT1, exits via ferroportin, then is oxidized (hephaestin or ceruloplasmin) for transferrin binding. A and B are mechanistically wrong. D is opposite: hepcidin causes ferroportin internalization and lowers plasma iron. Clinical relevance: core mechanism in iron-deficiency and inflammation questions.
questions.source: NEET PG Final Vitamins and Minerals Integration
questions.conceptTag: Iron absorption DMT1 ferroportin transferrin sequence
